title: Japanese Vegetable Korokke

overview.description: Crisp Japanese potato croquettes filled with sweetcorn, peas, carrot and onion, coated in panko breadcrumbs.

ingredients: 750g potatoes, peeled and cut into chunks
Oil, for shallow or deep frying
method: Boil the potatoes in salted water until completely tender.
Drain thoroughly and allow them to steam dry.
Mash the potatoes until mostly smooth.
Cook the carrots, peas and sweetcorn until tender.
Drain thoroughly.
Heat the oil in a frying pan.
Add the onion and cook until soft and lightly golden.
Add the soy sauce and cook for 30 seconds.
Add the carrots, peas and sweetcorn.
Cook for 2–3 minutes to remove excess moisture.
Add the vegetables to the mashed potatoes.
Season with black pepper and additional salt if needed.
Add the milk if the mixture seems too dry.
Mix thoroughly.
Allow the mixture to cool until easy to handle.
Shape the mixture into 10–12 flattened oval croquettes.
Chill for at least 30 minutes.
Coat each croquette in flour.
Dip into beaten egg.
Coat thoroughly with panko breadcrumbs.
Chill again for at least 30 minutes if possible.
Heat the oil to approximately 170–180°C.
Fry the korokke in batches until golden brown and crisp.
Drain on a wire rack.
Serve hot.

learning.did-you-know: Korokke developed from European-style croquettes introduced to Japan and became a popular Japanese comfort food in their own right.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a stable potato-based croquette and how chilling helps a breaded filling retain its shape during frying.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: An inexpensive, family-friendly vegetarian snack that freezes extremely well and uses vegetables already commonly kept in the freezer.
chapman-tips: Steam the potatoes dry after boiling so the mixture is not wet.
Cook the vegetables until tender but not waterlogged.
Cook the onions until lightly golden for extra flavour.
Allow the mixture to cool before shaping.
Chilling the shaped korokke makes them much easier to coat.
A second chill after breadcrumbing helps prevent the coating from falling off.
Panko gives a lighter, crispier coating than ordinary breadcrumbs.
The korokke can be made smaller for children's snacks.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes extremely well. Freeze the shaped and breadcrumb-coated korokke in a single layer before transferring them to a freezer bag or container.
notes.leftovers: Reheat in an oven or air fryer until hot and crisp.
